denial_letter_references.title: ProPublica — Uncovered (insurance denials reporting)
denial_letter_references.relevance: Calibrated P2 letter surface: Circular medical-necessity boilerplate.

denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

UnitedHealthcare has completed its medical necessity review of the request submitted by your provider for the levodopa-carbidopa intestinal gel (Duopa) infusion system to treat Parkinson disease with motor fluctuations. After review, the request is denied because the documentation submitted does not support medical necessity under the applicable UnitedHealthcare Medical Policy.

The UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines require documented failure of prerequisite lower-cost therapies before an intestinal gel infusion device will be authorized. The documentation submitted does not support that the required step-therapy options have been completed and failed. Before device-based therapy is authorized, the member must demonstrate an inadequate response to an optimized oral carbidopa-levodopa regimen, to a catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) inhibitor such as entacapone, and to a monoamine oxidase-B inhibitor. The documentation submitted does not support that these prerequisites are met, so the request does not meet the plan's coverage criteria at this time.

The clinical criteria applied in this determination are drawn from the plan's movement-disorders guideline developed for the geriatric population aged 65 and older. Under those geriatric criteria, advanced device-based therapy is reserved for patients who meet the thresholds defined for that age group, and the present request does not meet them.

Our records further indicate that a prior appeal concerning this service was closed as withdrawn at the member's request, and that matter is considered resolved.

If you disagree, you may request an internal appeal as described in your plan materials. Your treating provider may also contact UnitedHealthcare to request a peer-to-peer discussion. This determination addresses coverage under the terms of your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
UnitedHealthcare Clinical Services
clinical_context: The patient is a 48-year-old man with a nine-year history of Parkinson disease complicated by motor fluctuations, followed by a movement-disorders neurologist. Despite an optimized oral regimen of carbidopa-levodopa dosed eight times daily, the addition of entacapone, and a trial of the monoamine oxidase-B inhibitor rasagiline, he experiences approximately six hours of daily 'off' time with disabling bradykinesia and troublesome peak-dose dyskinesia. The documented failure of these optimized oral and adjunctive therapies satisfies the accepted prerequisites for advanced device-based therapy, contradicting the assertion that step-therapy options were not exhausted. At 48 he is decades younger than the geriatric population for whom the cited guideline was developed, so age-band thresholds drawn from patients aged 65 and older do not apply to him. His neurologist's records contain no request to withdraw any appeal; the prior appeal was filed by the treating practice and remained active, with no member-signed withdrawal on file.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches the assigned cell field-for-field (UHC, Medical Necessity, neurology, 41-55, M, step_therapy_missing); dx Parkinson disease with motor fluctuations (G20) is neurology; Duopa intestinal gel infusion is realistic for advanced PD; denial_rationale_seed faithfully describes step_therapy_missing (prerequisite lower-cost therapies not established); the two flaws are layered extras that do not displace the core step-therapy rationale; null employer_archetype acceptable for 41-55.

scenario_realism.reasoning: UHC denying Duopa (LCIG) as not medically necessary citing step_therapy_missing is an authentic utilization-management denial for high-cost device therapy; Duopa is FDA-approved for advanced PD with motor fluctuations; cited prerequisites (oral carbidopa-levodopa, entacapone/COMT, rasagiline/MAO-B) are real; the geriatric (65+) guideline misapplied to a 48-year-old and an appeal closed as 'withdrawn' with no documentation are believable, exploitable gaps.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Branded consistently as UnitedHealthcare, signs off 'UnitedHealthcare Clinical Services', cites the UHC-specific 'UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines', and repeatedly uses UHC's signature 'documentation does not support' cadence ('does not support medical necessity', 'does not support that these prerequisites are met'). A claims handler would readily believe this came from UHC; no mismatched tells.

denial_logic.reasoning: Authentically shoddy step_therapy_missing: asserts documentation does not support the prerequisite ladder (optimized oral levodopa, a COMT inhibitor, an MAO-B inhibitor) without specifying the evidence reviewed or what defines 'optimized', and the geriatric-criteria paragraph never states the actual thresholds; no hallucinated guideline; ample appeal room.

clinical_realism.reasoning: Nine-year PD with motor fluctuations producing ~6 hours daily off-time and peak-dose dyskinesia is internally consistent; carbidopa-levodopa dosed 8x/day, entacapone (COMT), and rasagiline (MAO-B) are real, correctly classed, and appropriately sequenced before device therapy; failed optimized oral/adjunctive therapy correctly qualifies for Duopa; no biology violations.

diagnosis_treatment_match.reasoning: Levodopa-carbidopa intestinal gel (Duopa) is FDA-approved and indicated specifically for advanced Parkinson disease with motor fluctuations, the exact stated condition; unambiguous standard-of-care category match.

diversity_delta.reasoning: No neighbour shares the Duopa device-aided intestinal infusion; the closest Parkinson neighbour (young-onset PD, multidisciplinary PT/OT under visit_limit_exceeded) is a different intervention and denial reason; the shared step_therapy_missing neighbour (focal epilepsy, lacosamide) is a different drug, dx, and trajectory; clearly distinct in feature-space.

diversity_statistical.reasoning: Max trigram-Jaccard: corpus=0.073 (nearest=case_170_aetna_priorauth), neighbours=0.0. Thresholds: warn>=0.35, fail>=0.5.

safety_redactor.reasoning: Adult 48M Parkinson device dispute; no banned topics (no SI/self-harm/abuse/IPV/EOL framing, not minor-gender care); UnitedHealthcare commercial plan with no Medicare/Medicaid framing; the geriatric-guideline reference is a clinical age-band criterion, not Medicare eligibility.

contradiction_hunter.reasoning: Gender (M), diagnosis (PD with motor fluctuations), and requested Duopa agree across letter and context; the three asserted prerequisites map exactly to the documented therapies (levodopa 8x/day, entacapone, rasagiline); the step-therapy-unmet, geriatric-criteria, and withdrawn-appeal claims are appealable falsehoods the context rebuts, not internal contradictions; coherent timeline.

llm_tell_detector.reasoning: Formal bureaucratic insurer boilerplate and a dense clinical recitation; prerequisites appear as an inline comma-separated clause, not a listicle; no AI tics, GPT vocabulary, or marketing cadence; no conspicuous tell at confidence >=0.7.

overall_tone.reasoning: Cold procedural UHC register ('the request is denied', 'does not meet the plan's coverage criteria at this time', 'that matter is considered resolved') with no apology or marketing; clinical context is plain analytic rebuttal prose, no pleading.

financial_auditor.reasoning: Silent on member out-of-pocket liability; no dollar figures; the only cost-adjacent phrasing ('prerequisite lower-cost therapies') is policy framing, not member liability; no Medicare/Medicaid conflation. A denial silent on cost liability is standard practice.

legal_auditor.reasoning: Internal appeal and provider peer-to-peer referenced; external-review/ERISA language and a numeric appeal window strategically omitted (a realistic attack vector, not a defect); no hallucinated statutes; no Medicare framing on this commercial UHC plan.

demographic_validator.reasoning: 48-year-old man with a nine-year PD history fits young-onset Parkinson (onset ~39); treatment matches diagnosis; movement-disorders neurologist appropriate; consistent male pronouns; no stereotyping. The geriatric-guideline mismatch is a clinical/policy flaw, not a demographic implausibility.

scope_guard.reasoning: UHC in-scope, fully_insured commercial, adult 48, member-facing internal appeal + peer-to-peer only, no Medicare/Medicaid/marketplace framing, no live-filing/attorney/court-representation language; the geriatric (65+) reference is a clinical-flaw element, not an out-of-scope coverage type.

date_sanity.reasoning: No explicit calendar dates and no appeal-window numbers, so nothing chronologically contradictory; relative timelines (nine-year PD history in a 48-year-old, levodopa 8x/day, ~6 hours off-time, a prior appeal sequenced before the determination) are coherent and realistic for 2026 operations.

citation_traceability.reasoning: Generic un-named umbrella references ('UnitedHealthcare Medical Policy', 'UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines', 'the plan's movement-disorders guideline ... aged 65 and older') with no edition/module/page and no fabricated alphanumeric code; realistically frustrating-but-vague, exactly the intended pattern.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Multiple independent winning vectors: the step-therapy prerequisites are documented as completed and failed (levodopa 8x/day, entacapone/COMT, rasagiline/MAO-B, ~6h off-time); the cited criteria are drawn from a geriatric (65+) guideline applied to a 48-year-old; and the claimed member-requested appeal withdrawal is contradicted by the absence of any member-signed withdrawal. No genuine strong defenses; each pillar collapses against the record.
exploitable_weaknesses: Step-therapy prerequisites are documented as completed and failed (optimized levodopa 8x/day, entacapone/COMT inhibitor, rasagiline/MAO-B inhibitor with ~6h daily off-time), contradicting the denial's claim they were not satisfied
Denial applies a geriatric (age 65+) guideline to a 48-year-old, making the cited age-band thresholds inapplicable
Claimed member-requested withdrawal of the prior appeal is false - no member-signed withdrawal on file and the practice-filed appeal remained active
strong_defenses: A step-therapy requirement before authorizing device-based intestinal gel infusion is a legitimate, recognized coverage criterion in principle
